summary: Stancil argues that the missing wave of public self-flagellation after the Atlanta murders isn’t a sign of progress but evidence of how racism adapts to preserve itself. He suggests that when anti-Asian racism isn’t widely recognized as structural, there’s no safe “systemic” shield for apologies, so blame shifts and the caste system stays intact. The core point is that racism in America is flexible and self-protecting, and its contradictions make it stronger, not weaker.
money_quotes: The only constant is the outcome.
Racism in America isn’t defined by an ordered set of principles, but is shapeshifting and infinitely flexible, able to take whatever form is necessary to perpetuate it.
Its apparent inconsistencies don’t undermine it, but make it stronger.
You cannot take it apart with reason. You cannot pound the shape out of water.
Race and caste are not logic. They're an ugly, violent lie.
key_insight: The absence of performative apologies after Atlanta reveals how racism’s power lies in its adaptability, not in consistent logic.
